source_metadata.title_raw: John Imperato - I-18-052 - May 11, 2018 - Del Mar

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
John hnperato 
2034 Santa Fe Avenue 
Del Mar, Ca. 92014 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-18-052 
Dear Mr. Imperato: 
May 11, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Because you seek general information and have not provided 
infonnation regarding any specific governmental decision, we are providing informal assistance.2 
Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. 
In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices 
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should 
contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Do the members of the City of Del Mar's ("City") Sea Level Rise Technical Advisory 
Committee ("STAC'') have a conflict of interest when voting on issues related to an advisory report 
that will be presented to the Del Mar City Council ("City Council") regarding coastal adaptation 
policies? 
CONCLUSION 
The members of ST AC do not have a conflict of interest when voting on issues related to the 
advisory report because they are not public officials under the Act as STAC does not possesses 
decisionmaking authority. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8!000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 IO through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3 ).) 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. 1-18-052 
Page No. 2 
You are one of approximately eight members of STAC and are seeking advice on behalf of 
STAC members Terry Gaasterland, Mark Handzel, Nancy Stoke, Robin Crabtree and yourself. 
Formed by a City Council resolution in 2015, STAC works with City staff and a technical 
consultant to oversee the preparation of an amendment to the City's Local Coastal Program, which 
will ultimately require certification by the California Coastal Commission, to address sea-level rise 
and coastal flooding impacts to several vulnerable areas of the City. Specifically, STAC will 
provide oversight and ensure the amendment process is open, inclusive and develops consensus 
amongst the many stakeholders involved. In addition, STAC will assist in the review of technical 
studies and sea-level rise planning scenarios; assist the community in prioritizing vulnerable coastal 
resources; and provide input and policy recommendations to the City Council. 
ST AC has been working for about three years to provide a written advisory report 
("Adaptation Plan") indicating various adaptation strategies to sea level rise to present to the City 
Council. STAC's recommended Adaptation Plan was approved by the Planning Commission 
without making any changes. That Adaptation Plan then went to the City Council. The City Council 
modified less than 10-15 percent of the content of that recommendation. The City Council was 
unable to reach an agreement on the modified less than I 0-15 percent. The City Council then 
requested to work with three STAC members to re-write the modified less than I 0-15 percent in an 
attempt to get at a final Adaptation Plan that both ST AC and the City Council will approve to be 
sent to the Coastal Commission. 
Some of the strategies that ST AC members have discussed and voted upon at ST AC public 
meetings may have a financial impact on STAC committee members that reside in the City. All but 
two of the eight members of STAG reside in the City. STAC members that reside in the City's 
beach and bluff zones may suffer a negative financial impact if certain adaptation strategies are 
presented to the Coastal Commission. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100 prohibits any 
public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her officiarposition 
to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A conflict of 
interest exists whenever a public official makes, participates in or uses his or her official position to 
influence a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one 
or more of his or her interests as specified by Section 8 7103. 
The threshold question in determining if the members of ST AC are subject to the Act's 
conflict-of-interest provisions is whether the members are "public officials." For purposes of the 
Act, Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a 
state or local government agency. The term member is further defined by Regulation 18700( c )(2), 
which states: 


File No. 1-18-052 
Page No. 3 
"(2) 'Member' does not include an individual who perfonns duties as part 
of a committee, board, commission, group, or other body that does not have 
decisionmaking authority. 
"(A) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body possesses 
decisionmaking authority whenever: 
"(i) It may make a final governmental decision; 
"(ii) It may compel or prevent a governmental decision either by reason of 
an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be 
overridden;or 
"(iii) It makes substantive recommendations and, over an extended period 
of time, those recommendations have been regularly approved without significant 
amendment or modification by another public official or governmental agency. 
"(B) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body does not 
possess decisionmaking authority if it is fonned or engaged for the sole purpose 
of researching a topic and preparing a report or recommendation for submission to 
another public official or governmental agency that has final decisionmaking 
authority, and does not meet any of the criteria set forth in subsection (2)(A)(i-
iii)." 
Thus, a committee is deemed to have decisiorunaking authority ifit (1) makes a final 
decision, (2) can compel or prevent a decision, or (3) makes substantive recommendations over an 
extended period of time that are regularly approved without significant amendment or modification. 
Additionally, a committee does not possess decisionmaking authority when it is fanned for the sole 
purpose of researching and preparing a report for submission another government agency that has 
final decisionmaking authority. 
If STAC has decisionmaking authority under any of the above tests, its members would be 
considered public officials and are subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions. Alternatively, 
ifSTAC does not have decisionmaking authority, its members are not considered public officials 
under the Act and are not subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions. 
Based upon the facts provided, ST AC was established to help draft recommendations for an 
amendment to the City's Local Coastal Program. You have stated that STAC does not take final 
action on matters before them, but merely makes recommendations to the City Council for the City 
Council'~ consideration in its recommendation to the Coastal Commission. Moreover, there is no 
indication that STAC can compel or prevent any governmental decision. The next question is 
whether ST AC has or will make substantive recommendations regularly approved by the City 
Council without significant amendment or modification. 
You have stated that STAC has made just one recommendation to the City Council since its 
inception. This recommendation was accepted with the exception ofless than 10-15 percent of its 


File No. 1-18-052 
Page No. 4 
content, and that less than 10-15 percent is in the process of being modified with the involvement of 
STAC and the City Council. 
Previously, we have advised new advisory bodies that they are in fact solely advisory until a 
history of recommendations has been established. (Simon Advice Letter, No. 1-04-013, 
citing Traverso Advice Letter, No. 1-01-124 and Ball Advice Letter, No. 1-89-671.) Without a 
history of recommendations being accepted by the City Council, STAC is solely an advisory 
committee with no decisionmaking authority and, at this time, its members are not public officials 
subject to the Act. Additionally, a single instance of acceptance of a recommendation without 
modification does not establish a record of regularly approved recommendations. 
We also note that based on the facts you have provided it appears ST AC may have been 
formed or engaged for the sole purpose of researching and preparing a report for submission to the 
City Council which makes the final decision on what report is presented to the Coastal 
Commission. If that is the case, ST AC also does not possess decisionmaking authority for this 
reason. 
Nonetheless, we must caution that members of ST AC could become public officials at a 
later date. If STAC's recommendations begin being regularly approved by the City Council over an 
extended period of time without significant modification or amendment by the City Council, you 
will ultimately need to revisit the issue of whether the ST AC may have decisionmaking authority. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
~
X~ /i~ 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
